title: Clinical trial designs in bfbin2arm
text: package supports several calibration modes determine operating characteristics must satisfy user-specified targets: Bayesian calibration: Bayesian power Bayesian type-error calibrated using design priors H1H_1 H0H_0. Frequentist calibration: Frequentist power frequentist type-error calibrated, power evaluated single fixed point alternative type-error null boundary. Hybrid calibration: Bayesian power combined frequentist type-error. Full calibration: Bayesian frequentist constraints must hold simultaneously. strongest form calibrated trial design. modes available single-arm two-stage designs via function design_singlearm_bf() underlying two-stage calibration via optimal_twostage_singlearm_bf().

title: Frequentist and hybrid calibration of one-stage ROPE-based designs for single-arm phase II trials
text: vignette introduces calibration one-stage ROPE-based designs single-arm phase II trials binary endpoints, implemented function design_singlearm_onestage_rope(). trial types, goal establish equivalence standard care success probability p0p_0 novel drug treatment. patient, failure success recorded single treatment arm, primary endpoint binary. offers flexibility wide range applications. design based region practical equivalence (ROPE) around benchmark response probability p0p_0. ROPE defined ℛp=[p0−δ,p0+δ]∩(0,1), \mathcal{R}_p = [p_0 - \delta,\; p_0 + \delta]\cap(0,1), δ>0\delta > 0 denotes half-width ROPE. Equivalence accepted posterior probability pp lies inside ROPE exceeds chosen threshold, Pr⁡(p∈ℛp∣Y=y)≥γeq. \Pr(p \\mathcal{R}_p \mid Y=y) \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{eq}}. Bayes-factor-based one-stage design, bfbin2arm provides four calibration modes ROPE designs: Bayesian: Calibrate Bayesian predictive power predictive type-error. Frequentist: Calibrate frequentist power fixed point alternative frequentist type-error. Hybrid: Calibrate Bayesian predictive power frequentist type-error. Full: Calibrate four operating characteristics simultaneously. addition, ROPE design three important tuning parameters influence frequentist operating characteristics: posterior probability threshold γeq\gamma_{\mathrm{eq}}, ROPE half-width δ\delta, point alternative dpdp frequentist power evaluated. vignette explains calibration modes illustrates impact parameters worked example.

code: des_bayes <- design_singlearm_onestage_rope(   n_min = 20,   n_max = 300,   p0 = p0,   delta = delta,   gamma_eq = 0.925,   a = a, b = b,   da0 = da0, db0 = db0,   da1 = da1, db1 = db1,   calibration = "Bayesian",   target_power = 0.80,   target_type1 = 0.10,   sustain_n = 10 ) des_bayes One-stage single-arm ROPE design Calibration: Bayesian  Search range n: 20 to 300  Null probability p0: 0.3  ROPE half-width delta: 0.12  Probability threshold gamma_eq: 0.925  Analysis prior: Beta(1, 1) Design prior (H0): Beta(60, 40) Design prior (H1): Beta(36, 84) Target Bayesian power: 0.8  Target Bayesian type-I error: 0.1  Sustain n: 10  Selected sample size n*: 173  Bayesian power(n*): 0.8166  Bayesian type-I(n*): 0.0001  Equivalence region: [39, 63] plot(des_bayes)

text: now provide simple example calibration function design_singlearm_onestage_rope(), calibrates single-arm one-stage phase II design using ROPE primary measure evidence. consider setting benchmark response rate p0=0.30p_0 = 0.30 regard differences 0.12 clinically negligible. Thus ROPE pp ℛp=[0.18,0.42]\mathcal{R}_p = [0.18, 0.42]. use: uniform analysis prior Beta(1,1)\mathrm{Beta}(1,1), non-equivalence design prior Beta(60,40)\mathrm{Beta}(60,40) mean 0.60, representing clearly superior response compared 0.30 (non-equivalence); design prior H0H_0 equivalence design prior Beta(36,84)\mathrm{Beta}(36,84) mean 0.30, representing plausible equivalence scenarios; design prior H1H_1 equivalence threshold γeq=0.80\gamma_{\mathrm{eq}} = 0.80, target ROPE-based power 0.80 equivalence design prior, maximum ROPE-based type-error 0.10 non-equivalence design prior, sustain requirement sustain_n = 10, meaning criteria must hold 10 consecutive sample sizes starting selected n*n^\ast. can take look resulting design object: printed output reports: search range nn, ROPE specification (p0, delta, gamma_eq), analysis design priors beta parameterization, target power type-constraints, chosen sustain_n, selected sample size Selected n, ROPE-based power type-error nn, equivalence decision region [ymin⁡eq,ymax⁡eq][y_{\min}^{\mathrm{eq}}, y_{\max}^{\mathrm{eq}}], .e. responder counts yy lead practical equivalence.
code: des_baseline <- design_singlearm_onestage_rope(   n_min = 20,   n_max = 200,   p0 = 0.30,     # benchmark response rate p0   delta = 0.12,  # ROPE half-width: equivalence if p in [0.18, 0.42]   gamma_eq = 0.80,  # posterior ROPE probability threshold for equivalence    # Analysis prior: p ~ Beta(a, b), used for posterior and ROPE decision   a = 1,   b = 1,    # Design prior under H0 (non-equivalence): p ~ Beta(da0, db0)   # Here: mean 0.60, representing clearly higher response than 0.30.   da0 = 60,   db0 = 40,    # Design prior under H1 (equivalence): p ~ Beta(da1, db1)   # Here: mean 0.30, representing plausible equivalence scenarios.   da1 = 36,   db1 = 84,    # Target ROPE-based power under H1 (equivalence design prior)   target_power = 0.80,    # Maximum ROPE-based type-I error under H0 (non-equivalence design prior)   target_type1 = 0.10,    # Stability requirement: criteria must hold for 10 consecutive n values   sustain_n = 10 ) des_baseline #> One-stage single-arm ROPE design #> Direction: equivalence  #> Calibration: Bayesian  #> Search range n: 20 to 200  #> Null probability p0: 0.3  #> Margin delta: 0.12  #> Probability threshold gamma_eq: 0.8  #> Probability threshold gamma_diff: 0.8  #> Analysis prior: Beta(1, 1) #> Design prior (H0): Beta(60, 40) #> Design prior (H1): Beta(36, 84) #> Target Bayesian power: 0.8  #> Target Bayesian type-I error: 0.1  #> Sustain n: 10  #> Selected sample size n*: 94  #> Bayesian power(n*): 0.8231  #> Bayesian type-I(n*): 0.0009  #> PCE(H0)(n*): 0.9730  #> Equivalence region: {20-35} #> Compelling evidence for non-equivalence region: {0-13, 44-94}

H_1:\; p \[\,p_0 - \delta,\; p_0 + \delta\,]      \quad \text{(practical equivalence)}. adopt following ROPE-based decision rule: Accept equivalence (H1H_1) Pr⁡(p∈ℛp∣y)≥γeq\Pr(p \\mathcal{R}_p \mid y) \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{eq}}. Accept non-equivalence (H0H_0) Pr⁡(p∉ℛp∣y)≥γdiff\Pr(p \notin \mathcal{R}_p \mid y) \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{diff}}. code: library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(purrr) library(ggplot2) library(knitr)  # Oncology-inspired equivalence example: revisited n_min <- 10 n_max <- 300 p0    <- 0.30  # ROPE and evidence thresholds delta      <- 0.12 gamma_eq   <- 0.925 gamma_diff <- 0.90  # Analysis prior a <- 1 b <- 1  # Design priors as in the first example da0 <- 60 db0 <- 40   # non-equivalence prior (H0)  da1 <- 36 db1 <- 84   # equivalence prior (H1)  # Calibration targets target_power      <- 0.80   # Bayesian predictive power under H1 target_type1      <- 0.10   # Bayesian predictive type-I error under H0 target_pce_h0     <- 0.80   # predictive compelling evidence for H0 target_freq_power <- 0.80   # frequentist power at dp (here dp = p0) target_freq_type1 <- 0.10   # frequentist type-I error at ROPE boundaries  # Point alternative for frequentist power dp <- p0  # Design calibration in "full" mode fit_pce_freq <- design_singlearm_onestage_rope(   n_min      = n_min,   n_max      = n_max,   p0         = p0,   delta      = delta,   gamma_eq   = gamma_eq,   gamma_diff = gamma_diff,   direction  = "equivalence",   a          = a,   b          = b,   da0        = da0,   db0        = db0,   da1        = da1,   db1        = db1,   calibration        = "full",   dp                 = dp,   target_power       = target_power,   target_type1       = target_type1,   target_pce_h0      = target_pce_h0,   target_freq_power  = target_freq_power,   target_freq_type1  = target_freq_type1,   sustain_n          = 10,   return_grid        = TRUE )  fit_pce_freq #> One-stage single-arm ROPE design #> Direction: equivalence  #> Calibration: full  #> Search range n: 10 to 300  #> Null probability p0: 0.3  #> Margin delta: 0.12  #> Probability threshold gamma_eq: 0.925  #> Probability threshold gamma_diff: 0.9  #> Analysis prior: Beta(1, 1) #> Design prior (H0): Beta(60, 40) #> Design prior (H1): Beta(36, 84) #> Target Bayesian power: 0.8  #> Target Bayesian type-I error: 0.1  #> Target PCE(H0): 0.8  #> Frequentist power point dp: 0.3  #> Target frequentist power: 0.8  #> Target frequentist type-I error: 0.1  #> Sustain n: 10  #> Selected sample size n*: 173  #> Bayesian power(n*): 0.8166  #> Bayesian type-I(n*): 0.0001  #> PCE(H0)(n*): 0.9846  #> Frequentist power(n*): 0.9597  #> Frequentist type-I(n*): 0.0784  #>  at p0 - delta: 0.0755  #>  at p0 + delta: 0.0784  #> Equivalence region: {39-63} #> Compelling evidence for non-equivalence region: {0-24, 81-173} library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(purrr) library(ggplot2) # Extract selected row and key operating characteristics sel <- fit_pce_freq$selected  summary_tab <- tibble(   quantity = c(     "Selected sample size n*",     "Bayesian power under H1 at n*",     "Bayesian type-I error under H0 at n*",     "PCE(H0) at n*",     "Frequentist power at p = p0",     "Frequentist type-I error (worst boundary)"   ),   value = c(     fit_pce_freq$n_star,     sel$power,     sel$type1,     sel$pce_h0,     sel$freq_power,     sel$freq_type1   ) )  kable(   summary_tab,   digits = 3,   col.names = c("Quantity", "Value"),   caption = "Operating characteristics of the calibrated equivalence design with constraints on Bayesian power, Bayesian type-I error, PCE(H0), and frequentist power/type-I error." ) des_onc_with_freq_power <- design_singlearm_onestage_rope(   n_min = 20,   n_max = 200,   p0 = 0.30,   delta = 0.12,   gamma_eq = 0.80,      # frequentist power at p = 0.3   dp = 0.3,    # Analysis prior p ~ Beta(a, b)   a = 1, b = 1,    # Design priors under H0 and H1   da0 = 60, db0 = 40,   # H0: non-equivalence, mean ~0.60   da1 = 36, db1 = 84,   # H1: equivalence, mean ~0.3    target_power = 0.80,   target_type1 = 0.10,   target_freq_type1 = 0.10,   target_freq_power = 0.80,   sustain_n = 10,   calibration = "Bayesian" )  sel_orig <- des_onc_with_freq_power$selected sel_new  <- fit_pce_freq$selected  comparison_tab <- tibble(   design = c("Bayesian (original)", "Full (Bayes + frequentist + PCE(H0))"),   n_star = c(sel_orig$n, fit_pce_freq$n),   bayes_power = c(sel_orig$power, sel_new$power),   bayes_type1 = c(sel_orig$type1, sel_new$type1),   pce_h0 = c(sel_orig$pce_h0, sel_new$pce_h0),   freq_power = c(sel_orig$freq_power, sel_new$freq_power),   freq_type1 = c(sel_orig$freq_type1, sel_new$freq_type1) )  kable(   comparison_tab,   digits = 3,   caption = "Comparison of the original Bayesian calibration and the extended design with additional constraints on PCE(H0) and frequentist power/type-I error." )

title: Optimal Bayesian calibration for single-arm two-stage Bayes factor designs
text: main calibration function design_singlearm_bf(). important arguments : n1_min: minimal interim sample size. n2_max: maximal final sample size. k: efficacy threshold. k_f: futility threshold. p0: null response probability. a0, b0: analysis-prior parameters (aa,ba)(a_a,b_a) used inside Bayes factor H0H_0. a1, b1: analysis-prior parameters (aa,ba)(a_a,b_a) used inside Bayes factor H1H_1. da0, db0: Bayesian design-prior parameters (ad,bd)(a_d,b_d) used Bayesian operating characteristics H0H_0. da1, db1: Bayesian design-prior parameters (ad,bd)(a_d,b_d) used Bayesian operating characteristics H1H_1. dp: fixed response probability used point alternative strictly frequentist power calculations. type: Bayes-factor type, currently "point" "direction". target_power: target Bayesian power. target_type1: target Bayesian type-error. target_ce_h0: optional lower bound probability compelling evidence H0H_0. power_cushion: optional additional margin used fixed-sample calibration introducing interim analysis. Serves primarily allow algorithm find optimal design. function returns object class singlearm_bf_design containing selected design, corrected operating characteristics, search results candidate interim sample sizes.

fixed_diag_full <- function(n) {   tmp <- bfbin2arm:::singlearm_fixed_oc(     n    = n,     k    = 1/3,     p0   = 0.2,     a0   = 1, b0 = 1,     a1   = 1, b1 = 1,     da0  = 1, db0 = 1,     da1  = 2.5, db1 = 2,     dp   = 0.4,     type = "direction",     k_ce = 3   )   c(     n          = n,     pfineff    = tmp$pfineff,     pfineff0   = tmp$pfineff0,     pce0_corr  = tmp$pce0_corr,     ok         =       !is.na(tmp$pfineff)  &&       !is.na(tmp$pfineff0) &&       !is.na(tmp$pce0_corr) &&       tmp$pfineff  >= (0.80 + 0.025) &&       tmp$pfineff0 <= 0.05           &&       tmp$pce0_corr >= 0.80   ) }  n_full <- 6:200 fd_full <- t(vapply(n_full, fixed_diag_full, numeric(5))) colnames(fd_full) <- c("n","pfineff","pfineff0","pce0_corr","ok")  plot(fd_full[, "n"], fd_full[, "pfineff"],      type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "darkorange",      ylim = c(0, 1),      xlab = "n", ylab = "Value",      main = "Fixed-sample directional OC vs n")  lines(fd_full[, "n"], fd_full[, "pfineff0"], lwd = 2, col = "steelblue") lines(fd_full[, "n"], fd_full[, "pce0_corr"], lwd = 2, col = "darkmagenta")  abline(h = 0.80 + 0.025, lty = 2, col = "darkorange")   # target power + cushion abline(h = 0.05,           lty = 2, col = "steelblue")   # target type-I abline(h = 0.80,           lty = 2, col = "darkmagenta") # target CE(H0)  legend("bottomright",        legend = c("Bayesian power", "Bayesian type-I", "CE(H0)"),        col = c("darkorange", "steelblue", "darkmagenta"),        lwd = 2, bty = "n")

text: Hybrid calibration naturally depends H1H_1 design prior Bayesian power prior-predictive quantity. can explore varying da1 db1 keeping settings fixed. table shows optimal design operating characteristics change H1H_1 design prior. particular: concentrated priors around optimistic response rates can lead smaller sample sizes higher Bayesian power, priors substantial mass near p0p_0 may require larger sample sizes reach power target.
code: prior_grid <- list(   list(da1 = 1.5, db1 = 1.0, label = "weakly informative"),   list(da1 = 2.5, db1 = 2.0, label = "moderately informative"),   list(da1 = 4.0, db1 = 2.5, label = "more concentrated") )  extract_row <- function(res, label) {   if (is.null(res) || isFALSE(res$feasible)) {     return(data.frame(       prior_label = label,       feasible    = FALSE,       n1          = NA_integer_,       n2          = NA_integer_,       power       = NA_real_,       type1       = NA_real_,       freq_power  = NA_real_,       freq_type1  = NA_real_,       en_h0       = NA_real_,       en_h1       = NA_real_     ))   }    oc <- res$operating_characteristics   data.frame(     prior_label = label,     feasible    = res$feasible,     n1          = res$design["n1"],     n2          = res$design["n2"],     power       = oc$power,     type1       = oc$type1,     freq_power  = oc$freq_power,     freq_type1  = oc$freq_type1,     en_h0       = oc$en_h0,     en_h1       = oc$en_h1   ) }  hybrid_sensitivity <- lapply(prior_grid, function(pr) {   fit <- tryCatch(     design_singlearm_bf(       n1_min = 5,       n2_max = 100,       k      = 1/10,       k_f    = 3,       p0     = 0.2,       a0     = 1,       b0     = 1,       a1     = 1,       b1     = 1,       dp     = 0.4,       da0    = 1,       db0    = 1,       da1    = pr$da1,       db1    = pr$db1,       type   = "direction",       calibration       = "hybrid",       target_power      = 0.80,       target_freq_type1 = 0.05,       power_cushion = 0.025     ),     error = function(e) NULL   )    extract_row(fit, pr$label) })  hybrid_sensitivity <- do.call(rbind, hybrid_sensitivity) knitr::kable(hybrid_sensitivity, digits = 3)

title: Optimal Bayesian calibration of two-arm two-stage Bayes factor designs with binary endpoints
text: consider two-arm trial control arm (arm 1) treatment arm (arm 2). Let p1p_1 p2p_2 denote response probabilities two arms. typical hypothesis setup : H0:p1=p2H_0: p_1 = p_2, H1:p1≠p2H_1: p_1 \neq p_2. Bayes factor BF01BF_{01} compares marginal likelihood H0H_0 H1H_1. Small values BF01BF_{01} (e.g. BF01<1/3BF_{01} < 1/3 BF01<1/10BF_{01} < 1/10) indicate evidence H0H_0, whereas large values (e.g. BF01≥3BF_{01} \ge 3) indicate evidence favor H0H_0. Using difference parameter η=p2−p1\eta=p_2-p_1, typical hypothesis setups phase II trial : H0:η≤0 versus H1:η>0H_0:\eta \leq 0 \hspace{1cm} \text{ versus } \hspace{1cm} H_1:\eta > 0 H0:η=0 versus H1:η>0H_0:\eta = 0 \hspace{1cm} \text{ versus } \hspace{1cm} H_1:\eta > 0 H0:η=0 versus H1:η<0H_0:\eta = 0 \hspace{1cm} \text{ versus } \hspace{1cm} H_1:\eta < 0 details explanations directional tests, see (Kelter 2026b). associated Bayes factors three directional tests denoted BF+−\mathrm{BF}_{+-}, BF+0\mathrm{BF}_{+0} BF−0\mathrm{BF}_{-0}. Also, denote H−:η≤0H_-:\eta \leq 0 H+:η>0H_+:\eta > 0.

Two-stage calibration (step 2): Conditional fixed-sample design, considers admissible interim sample sizes (n11,n12)(n_1^1, n_1^2) grid , candidate, computes corrected operating characteristics. Among satisfy constraints, selects design minimizes expected sample size H0H_0. number interim designs considered step 2 #{interim designs}=#{n11}×#{n12}, \#\{\text{interim designs}\} = \#\{n_1^1\} \times \#\{n_1^2\}, , arm jj, admissible interim sample sizes form grid {n1j}={n1,minj,n1,minj+𝚐𝚛𝚒𝚍_𝚜𝚝𝚎𝚙,…,n2j−1} \{n_1^j\} = \{n_{1,\min}^j,\, n_{1,\min}^j + \texttt{grid\_step},\, \dots,\, n_{2}^j - 1\} applying interim_fraction bounds. Thus #{n1j}\#\{n_1^j\} number grid points lower upper interim limits arm jj, product bounds. title: Optimal Bayesian calibration of two-arm two-stage Bayes factor designs with binary endpoints
text: main calibration function two-stage design design_twoarm_twostage_bf(). Internally, calls lower-level engine optimal_twostage_2arm_bf() perform two-step search (fixed-sample anchor, interim-grid search shown Figure 1), users typically interact design function. Bayesian workflow considered vignette, important arguments design_twoarm_twostage_bf() : k, k_f: Efficacy futility thresholds Bayes factor. Evidence null declared Bayes factor falls k, compelling evidence null declared Bayes factor exceeds k_f. n1_min, n2_max: Vectors length 2 giving minimal interim maximal final sample sizes two arms. alloc1, alloc2: Allocation probabilities two arms; must positive sum 1. target_power, target_type1: Target corrected Bayesian power type-error. Internally determine alpha beta fixed-sample anchor search. target_ce_h0: Optional lower bound corrected Bayesian probability compelling evidence null hypothesis. target_freq_power, target_freq_type1: Optional targets frequentist power type-error; can activated via calibration mode. calibration: Calibration criterion, one "Bayesian", "frequentist", "hybrid". vignette focus calibration = "Bayesian". calibration_en: Criterion ranking designs expected sample size, either "Bayesian" (default) "frequentist". power_cushion: Optional extra power margin used step 1 identifying sufficient fixed-sample design. relevant Kelter (2026b) showed power two-arm design can decrease introducing interim analysis allows stopping futility . consequence, power cushion safeguards obtaining design meet required power target introducing interim analysis. interim_fraction: Lower upper bounds interim sample sizes, expressed fractions fixed-sample sizes found step 1. Defaults c(0, 1), means interim designs n1_min fixed-sample size found step one calibration algorithm analysed. example, interim_fraction = c(0.25, 0.75) restricts interim look occur 25% 75% fixed-sample size, regardless numerical value n1_min. grid_step: Spacing interim-design grid searched step 2. coarse_step: Spacing used coarse fixed-sample search step 1. max_iter: Maximum number fixed-sample sizes explored step 1. progress: Logical; TRUE, prints progress messages calibration. helpful large number two-stage designs needs analyzed algorithm. test: Bayes-factor test "BF01", "BF+0", "BF-0" "BF+-". prior parameters a_0_d, b_0_d, a_0_a, b_0_a, … specify design analysis priors relevant hypotheses, exactly one-stage setting. function returns object class "twoarm_twostage_bf_design" following main components: design: Named vector c(n1_1, n1_2, n2_1, n2_2) giving interim sample sizes n1_1, n1_2 final sample sizes n2_1, n2_2. fixed_design: fixed-sample anchor identified step 1, stored c(n_fixed_1, n_fixed_2). operating_characteristics: Corrected two-stage operating characteristics trial design, accounting early stopping futility (Bayesian power type-error, CE(H0), , requested, frequentist operating characteristics expected sample sizes). fixed_operating_characteristics: Operating characteristics fixed-sample anchor step 1. inputs: list summarising inputs used determine design. optimizer: list containing convergence flag conv prior specification used internal engine. engine_output: full list returned internal optimizer optimal_twostage_2arm_bf(), retained transparency advanced use. Bayesian workflow, corrected operating characteristics operating_characteristics key output, quantify actual two-stage design rather fixed-sample surrogate used step 1. fixed-sample quantities fixed_design fixed_operating_characteristics primarily useful comparator. Three convenient methods provided: print() gives concise textual summary selected design operating characteristics. summary() adds search-level information explicit calibration targets. plot() produces six-panel base R plot, showing design schematic, operating characteristics, design analysis priors relevant hypotheses.

ICT-107 trial (immunologic response) ##    Placebo (control): 12 responders, 31 non-responders ##    ICT-107 (treatment): 49 responders, 32 non-responders   ## -------------------------------------------------------------  y1_ict <- 12      # control successes n1_ict <- 12 + 31 y2_ict <- 49      # treatment successes n2_ict <- 49 + 32  cat("\n=== ICT-107 Trial (n1 =", n1_ict, ", n2 =", n2_ict, ") ===\n") #>  #> === ICT-107 Trial (n1 = 43 , n2 = 81 ) ===  # BF01 BF01_ict = twoarmbinbf01(y1_ict, y2_ict, n1_ict, n2_ict,                           a_0_a = 1, b_0_a = 1,                           a_1_a = 1, b_1_a = 1,                           a_2_a = 1, b_2_a = 1)  # BF+1 BFp1_ict = BFplus1(y1_ict, y2_ict, n1_ict, n2_ict,                     a_1_a = 1, b_1_a = 1,                     a_2_a = 1, b_2_a = 1)  # BF-1 BFm1_ict = BFminus1(y1_ict, y2_ict, n1_ict, n2_ict,                      a_1_a = 1, b_1_a = 1,                      a_2_a = 1, b_2_a = 1)  # BF+0 cat("=== ICT-107 Trial === Bayes factor BF+0 results in ", BFplus0(BFp1_ict, BF01_ict)) #> === ICT-107 Trial === Bayes factor BF+0 results in  186.6192  # BF+- cat("=== ICT-107 Trial === Bayes factor BF+- results in ", BFplusMinus(BFp1_ict, BFm1_ict)) #> === ICT-107 Trial === Bayes factor BF+- results in  3702.659

code: des_informative <- design_twoarm_onestage_bf(   n_min = 10,   n_max = 100,   k = 1/30,   k_f = 30,   test = "BF+-",   calibration = "Bayesian",   target_power = 0.80,   target_type1 = 0.05,   target_ce_h0 = 0.60,   # design and analysis priors: flat Beta(1,1) everywhere   a_0_d = 1, b_0_d = 1,   a_0_a = 1, b_0_a = 1,   a_1_d = 1, b_1_d = 2,   a_2_d = 2, b_2_d = 1,   a_1_a = 1, b_1_a = 1,   a_2_a = 1, b_2_a = 1,   # assumed true proportions for frequentist power (optional here)   p1_power = 0.3, p2_power = 0.6,   # report frequentist type-I-error? (optional here)   report_freq_type1 = TRUE,   # equal randomisation   alloc1 = 0.5,   alloc2 = 0.5,   # require sustained feasibility over the next 10 larger n   sustain_n = 10L,   progress = FALSE ) summary(des_informative) Summary: One-stage two-arm Bayes factor design --------------------------------------------- Mode:        optimal Status:      Smallest feasible one-stage two-arm design found. title: Bayesian calibration of two-arm one-stage Bayes factor designs with binary endpoints
text: vignette illustrated design calibrate two‑arm one‑stage Bayes factor trials binary endpoints using bfbin2arm package. core workflow starts specifying Bayes factor test (two‑sided directional), choosing coherent design analysis priors competing hypotheses, mapping clinical requirements onto calibration targets Bayesian power, Bayesian type‑error, Bayesian probability compelling evidence null (H−H_- directional tests). central calibration function design_twoarm_onestage_bf() searches user‑defined grid total sample sizes returns design object contains selected allocation (n1,n2)(n_1, n_2), corresponding total sample size ntotaln_{\text{total}}, Bayesian frequentist operating characteristics chosen design. key innovation use sustained feasibility constraint, controlled argument sustain_n, guards oscillatory behaviour operating characteristics driven discreteness binomial model. Instead treating sample size feasible soon meets calibration thresholds pointwise, algorithm accepts candidate ntotaln_{\text{total}} relevant targets hold ntotaln_{\text{total}} continue hold least next sustain_n larger total sample sizes within search range. diagnostic plots reflect logic: operating characteristic (Bayesian power, Bayesian type‑error, CE(H0), optional frequentist power), vertical reference line drawn first total sample size corresponding metric attains target sustained sense. result, graphical summaries numerical design recommendations aligned directly interpretable robust local oscillations operating characteristic curves. Using ICT‑107 phase II trial running example, shown flat design priors can replaced informative priors encode realistic expectations treatment control response rates. shift often allows one (1) achieve desired calibration targets substantially smaller total sample sizes compared flat priors (2) achieve higher constraints certain operating characteristics probability compelling evidence identical sample sizes, especially strong evidence thresholds (e.g. k=1/30k = 1/30, kf=30k_f = 30) required. vignette also demonstrated handle equal unequal randomization, request frequentist type‑error power alongside Bayesian criteria, interpret resulting design recommendations terms total sample size ntotaln_{\text{total}} arm‑specific allocations. Overall, bfbin2arm package provides flexible, unified framework Bayesian, frequentist, hybrid, fully dual calibrations can performed visualised way directly tied clinically meaningful decision thresholds. details methodology can found (Kelter 2026).

text: consider two-arm trial control arm (arm 1) treatment arm (arm 2). Let p1p_1 p2p_2 denote response probabilities two arms. typical hypothesis setup : H0:p1=p2H_0: p_1 = p_2, H1:p1≠p2H_1: p_1 \neq p_2. Bayes factor BF01BF_{01} compares marginal likelihood H0H_0 H1H_1. Small values BF01BF_{01} (e.g. BF01<1/3BF_{01} < 1/3 BF01<1/10BF_{01} < 1/10) indicate evidence H0H_0, whereas large values (e.g. BF01≥3BF_{01} \ge 3) indicate evidence favor H0H_0. Using difference parameter η=p2−p1\eta=p_2-p_1, typical hypothesis setups phase II trial : H0:η≤0 versus H1:η>0H_0:\eta \leq 0 \hspace{1cm} \text{ versus } \hspace{1cm} H_1:\eta > 0 H0:η=0 versus H1:η>0H_0:\eta = 0 \hspace{1cm} \text{ versus } \hspace{1cm} H_1:\eta > 0 H0:η=0 versus H1:η<0H_0:\eta = 0 \hspace{1cm} \text{ versus } \hspace{1cm} H_1:\eta < 0 details explanations directional tests, see [@kelter_power_2026]. associated Bayes factors three directional tests denoted BF+−\mathrm{BF}_{+-}, BF+0\mathrm{BF}_{+0} BF−0\mathrm{BF}_{-0}. Also, denote H−:η≤0H_-:\eta \leq 0 H+:η>0H_+:\eta > 0.

title: NA
text: main design function optimal_twostage_2arm_bf(). Bayesian workflow considered vignette, important arguments : alpha: target Bayesian type-error. beta: target Bayesian type-II error, target Bayesian power 1 - beta. k: efficacy threshold. Evidence null hypothesis declared Bayes factor falls k. k_f: futility threshold. Compelling evidence null hypothesis declared Bayes factor least k_f. n1_min: vector length 2 giving minimal interim sample sizes two arms. n2_max: vector length 2 giving maximal final sample sizes two arms. alloc1, alloc2: allocation probabilities two arms. power_cushion: optional extra power margin used step 1 identifying sufficient fixed-sample design. pceH0: optional lower bound probability compelling evidence favour null hypothesis. interim_fraction: lower upper bounds interim sample sizes, expressed fractions fixed-sample sizes found step 1. Defaults c(0,1), means interim designs n1_min fixed-sample size found step 1 calibration algorithm analyzed. , example, interim_fraction = c(0.25,0.75), minimum sample size interim analysis starts 25% fixed-sample size found step 1 calibration algorithm, matter value n1_min takes. Likewise, maximum sample size interim analysis point 75% fixed-sample size. grid_step: spacing interim-design grid searched step 2. coarse_step: spacing used coarse fixed-sample search step 1. progress: logical; TRUE, prints progress messages search. max_iter: maximal number total sample sizes explored step 1. calibration_mode: calibration criterion. vignette use calibration_mode = "Bayesian" throughout. Future versions package include options fully frequentist calibration hybrid calibration mode. test: Bayes-factor test "BF01", "BF+0", "BF-0" "BF+-". function returns list following main components: design: vector c(n1_1, n1_2, n2_1, n2_2) giving interim sample sizes n1_1 n1_2 final sample sizes n2_1 n2_2. naive_oc: fixed-sample operating characteristics step 1. occ: corrected two-stage operating characteristics trial design, accounting possible early stopping futility. priors: list containing prior specification search settings. conv: convergence flag describing whether feasible design found. Typical values include "converged", "no_feasible_fixed", "no_interim_grid", "no_feasible_design". Explanations follow example. freq_occ: optional frequentist operating characteristics. component used Bayesian vignette therefore discussed . Future versions package include fully frequentist calibration, . Bayesian workflow, corrected operating characteristics occ key output, quantify actual two-stage design rather fixed-sample surrogate found step 1 calibration algorithm.

title: NA
text: calibration algorithm optimal_twostage_2arm_bf() proceeds two steps: Fixed-sample calibration (step 1): searches total sample sizes find sufficient fixed-sample design (n21,n22)(n_2^1, n_2^2) meets target power Pr⁡(BF01<k∣H1)\Pr(\mathrm{BF}_{01}<k\mid H_1), type-error Pr⁡(BF01<k∣H0)\Pr(\mathrm{BF}_{01}<k\mid H_0) (optionally) probability compelling evidence null hypothesis Pr⁡(BF01>kf∣H0)\Pr(\mathrm{BF}_{01}>k_f\mid H_0). Two-stage calibration (step 2): Conditional fixed-sample design, considers admissible interim sample sizes (n11,n12)(n_1^1, n_1^2) grid , candidate, computes corrected operating characteristics. Among satisfy constraints, selects design minimizes expected sample size H0H_0. number interim designs considered step 2 #{interim designs}=#{n11}×#{n12}, \#\{\text{interim designs}\} = \#\{n_1^1\} \times \#\{n_1^2\}, arm’s interim range bounded n1_min (interim_fraction[1] * n_2^j) n_2^j - 1 (interim_fraction[2] * n_2^j), discretised grid_step. Thus, larger sufficient fixed-sample size found step 1, larger grid interim designs explored step 2, longer runtime. Several modelling choices strongly influence runtime, provide details discussing first example. turn first detailed example now, showing calibrate Bayesian phase II design practice function optimal_twostage_2arm_bf().

Typical remedies relax constraints (e.g. increase alpha, relax pceH0, reduce 1 - beta) increase n2_max max_iter. "no_interim_grid" fixed-sample design found step 1, admissible interim-sample region implied n1_min, n2_max, interim_fraction empty. words, pair (n1(1),n1(2))(n_1^{(1)}, n_1^{(2)}) lies strictly (n2(1),n2(2))(n_2^{(1)}, n_2^{(2)}) satisfies interim-range constraints. case algorithm construct two-stage candidates. Adjusting n1_min widening interim_fraction usually resolves . "no_feasible_design" step 1 step 2 ran, least one interim grid evaluated, two-stage design grid satisfies specified constraints power, type-error, (optionally) pceH0. function returns NA design corrected operating characteristics case. obtain feasible design, one can enlarge search space (e.g. increase n2_max allow finer grid via grid_step) relax Bayesian constraints.

Passed optimal_twostage_2arm_bf(). p1_en_h0, p2_en_h0 Optional null response probabilities used calibration_en = "frequentist" compute expected sample size null. p_null_grid Optional grid null response probabilities used frequentist type--error maximisation. progress Logical; TRUE, print simple progress information calibration. ... Target corrected frequentist power dp. target_freq_type1 Numeric scalar \((0,1)\). Target corrected frequentist type-error \(p = p_0\). power_cushion Non-negative numeric scalar. Optional additive cushion applied power targets calibration. k_ce Optional numeric scalar greater 1. Threshold \(BF_{01}\) scale used CE(H0) / PCE(H0) calculations. sustain_n Non-negative integer. candidate sample size declared feasible relevant constraints satisfied sample size next sustain_n larger sample sizes, subject search range.

Typical values include "converged", "no_feasible_fixed", "no_interim_grid", "no_feasible_design". frequentist hybrid calibration modes, additional informative status values may returned best available design returned although requested constraints fully satisfied.

code: ## Fast Bayesian example with small search space res <- optimal_twostage_2arm_bf(   alpha = 0.10,   beta = 0.20,   k = 1 / 3,   k_f = 3,   n1_min = c(3, 3),   n2_max = c(12, 12),   alloc1 = 0.5,   alloc2 = 0.5,   power_cushion = 0,   pceH0 = NULL,   interim_fraction = c(0.25, 0.75),   grid_step = 2L,   coarse_step = 4L,   progress = FALSE,   max_iter = 24L,   calibration_mode = "Bayesian",   test = "BF01",   a_0_d = 1, b_0_d = 1,   a_0_a = 1, b_0_a = 1,   a_1_d = 1, b_1_d = 1,   a_2_d = 1, b_2_d = 1,   a_1_a = 1, b_1_a = 1,   a_2_a = 1, b_2_a = 1 ) res$design #> [1] NA NA NA NA res$occ #> NULL  # \donttest{ res2 <- optimal_twostage_2arm_bf(   alpha = 0.05,   beta = 0.20,   k = 1 / 3,   k_f = 3,   n1_min = c(5, 5),   n2_max = c(20, 20),   alloc1 = 0.5,   alloc2 = 0.5,   power_cushion = 0.02,   pceH0 = 0.50,   interim_fraction = c(0.25, 0.75),   grid_step = 1L,   coarse_step = 4L,   progress = FALSE,   max_iter = 40L,   calibration_mode = "Bayesian",   test = "BF+0",   a_0_d = 1, b_0_d = 1,   a_0_a = 1, b_0_a = 1,   a_1_d = 1, b_1_d = 2,   a_2_d = 2, b_2_d = 1,   a_1_a = 1, b_1_a = 1,   a_2_a = 1, b_2_a = 1,   a_1_d_Hminus = 1, b_1_d_Hminus = 1,   a_2_d_Hminus = 1, b_2_d_Hminus = 1,   a_1_a_Hminus = 1, b_1_a_Hminus = 1,   a_2_a_Hminus = 1, b_2_a_Hminus = 1 ) res2$design #> [1] NA NA NA NA res2$occ #> NULL # }
